exercises.explain: In standard training, a discriminator gets too confident seeing only perfect 0s and 1s, which stops the generator from learning.
exercises.prompt: What happens when a discriminator becomes 100% confident too early in GAN training?

options.text: The discriminator becomes too strong and stops providing useful gradients.
The generator becomes too smart and trains too quickly.
The training data gets completely deleted.

cards.body: Imagine playing a game where the referee is a genius and you are just starting. If the ref instantly penalizes your every move with zero feedback, you will never learn how to play!
cards.title: The Overpowered Referee
Softening the Rules
cards.body: Label Smoothing and Noisy Labels are tricks that soften the targets for the discriminator. Instead of hard 0 and 1 targets, we use soft numbers like 0.9 or occasionally flip the labels randomly.

cards.title: The Kind Teacher
cards.body: Think of a teacher grading a test. Instead of only giving absolute 0% or 100% scores, they give a 90% or a 95%. This keeps students motivated to improve without getting crushed by perfectionism.
When training a GAN to draw cats, instead of telling the discriminator 'Real images are exactly 1.0', we tell it 'Real images are 0.9'. This stops it from being too sure of itself.
cards.title: Cats at 90%
Fuzzy But Better
cards.body: You might think making labels fuzzy makes the model less accurate. Actually, it prevents the discriminator from becoming a know-it-all, which helps the generator keep learning!

left.text: Real is 1.0, Fake is 0.0. Discriminator easily overpowers generator.

right.text: Real is 0.9, Fake is 0.1. Generator keeps receiving useful feedback.

options.text: It deletes half the training dataset to speed up training.
It softens target values to stop the discriminator from becoming too confident.
It makes the generator produce blurred images on purpose.

exercises.explain: Label smoothing replaces absolute targets (0 and 1) with slightly softer values (like 0.9) to prevent extreme confidence.
exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of using label smoothing in GANs?
True or False: Noisy labels involve occasionally flipping real and fake labels during training.
exercises.explain: Noisy labels mean we occasionally flip the labels (e.g., calling a fake image real) to challenge the discriminator and keep it on its toes.

exercises.prompt: Softening target labels prevents the discriminator from overpowering the generator and causing __________ gradients.
exercises.sentence: Softening target labels prevents the discriminator from overpowering the generator and causing [blank] gradients.
exercises.explain: If the discriminator is too confident, gradients drop to zero. Softening labels prevents these vanishing gradients.

exercises.prompt: Which case represents the successful application of label smoothing in GAN training?
exercises.explain: Case B shows the effect of label smoothing, which keeps the discriminator challenged so it continues guiding the generator.

options.text: Case A: The discriminator immediately achieves 100% accuracy and the generator stops improving entirely.
Case B: The discriminator is kept slightly uncertain, allowing the generator to steadily improve its image quality.

exercises.prompt: How does changing target labels from 1.0 to 0.9 save the generator from stalling?
exercises.explain: By using soft labels like 0.9, the discriminator never achieves absolute certainty, preserving the gradients the generator needs to learn. Up next: more stabilizing techniques for GANs!

options.text: It keeps gradients flowing to the generator by preventing 100% confidence.
It forces the generator to use less memory during training.
It automatically labels unlabeled internet images.

concept: Softening target labels (0.9 instead of 1.0) or flipping them randomly keeps the discriminator from overpowering the generator and causing vanishing gradients.
title: Label Smoothing & Noisy Labels
